questions.question_text: How long must a Montana broker retain transaction documents?
questions.explanation: Per ARM 24.210.414(1) (effective August 23, 2025, carrying forward the rule formerly in ARM 24.210.601), Montana licensees must maintain trust account records and real estate related documents — sales contracts, offers, leases and options, agency agreements, and closing statements — for 8 years from the date of receipt or the date the transaction was completed, whichever is latest. The 8-year period matches the statute of limitations on written contracts under MCA 27-2-202(1). Records may be paper or electronic and must be readily available for inspection.

questions.question_text: A salesperson leaves Broker Quinn's office. Who is responsible for retaining that salesperson's transaction records?
questions.explanation: In Montana, the supervising broker is responsible for retaining all transaction documents, even those created by salespersons who have since left the office — for the full 8-year retention period (ARM 24.210.414). Under ARM 24.210.607(1)(b), when supervision ends, listings and pending transactions remain the supervising broker's responsibility, and on request the broker must provide the former salesperson a statement documenting that salesperson's transactions from the preceding three years.

questions.question_text: Can a Montana broker store transaction records electronically?
distractors: No, only paper originals are accepted
questions.explanation: Montana permits electronic storage of transaction documents. Whether paper or electronic, all records must be readily producible for inspection and auditing throughout the required 8-year retention period under ARM 24.210.414 (which replaced former ARM 24.210.601 in 2025).

questions.question_text: A listing agreement is terminated by the seller. What must the broker do with the transaction documents?
distractors: Hold them until a new listing is signed
Send them to the Board for archival
questions.explanation: When a listing is terminated, the Montana broker provides the seller copies of the relevant transaction documents. The underlying rule is MCA 37-51-321(1)(o): it is unprofessional conduct to fail to voluntarily furnish a copy of a written instrument to a party at the time the party executes it. The broker must also retain their own copies for the 8-year retention period (ARM 24.210.414).

questions.question_text: The Board of Realty Regulation requests transaction files from a broker. How quickly must the broker make them available?
questions.correct_answer: Available for inspection at all times
questions.explanation: Montana brokers must keep trust account and transaction records organized so they can be produced whenever the Board or department examines them — ARM 24.210.427 requires trust account records to be 'maintained to facilitate auditing,' and the department may issue citations for trust account violations (MCA 37-51-324). No rule gives brokers a 30- or 60-day production window or limits review to annual audits.

questions.question_text: Who may hold a trust account for real estate transactions in Montana?
questions.explanation: Montana trust accounts must be in the broker's name only. Salespersons, office managers, and other staff cannot hold trust accounts. The broker bears sole responsibility for trust funds.

questions.question_text: A Montana broker opens a trust account at an out-of-state online bank. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, must be at a Montana financial institution
distractors: Yes, if the bank has a Montana branch
Only with written Board of Realty approval
questions.explanation: Montana requires trust accounts to be insured and held in Montana financial institutions (ARM 24.210.427(2)). An out-of-state online bank, even if FDIC-insured, does not satisfy this requirement.

questions.question_text: A Montana broker keeps $150 of personal funds in the trust account to cover bank service fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, up to $1,000 of personal funds is allowed
distractors: No, the maximum is $100 for bank fees
No, zero personal funds are permitted
Yes, but only with prior Board approval
questions.explanation: Under ARM 24.210.427(6), a Montana broker may deposit and hold up to $1,000 of personal funds in the trust account, including interest accrued to the broker. These funds may be used for trust account bank charges and related maintenance expenses. Keeping $150 is well within the limit; exceeding $1,000 would violate the rule.

questions.question_text: What designation must appear on a Montana broker's trust account?
questions.explanation: ARM 24.210.427(2) requires trust accounts to be insured, held in Montana financial institutions, and identified as trust accounts. This distinguishes them from operating or personal accounts and protects client funds.

questions.question_text: Broker Harris deposits her personal commission check into the trust account alongside a client's earnest money. What violation is this?
questions.explanation: Depositing personal funds into a trust account is commingling, which is prohibited in Montana. The only exception is up to $1,000 of the broker's personal funds (ARM 24.210.427(6)). Money due the broker — like an earned commission — must be withdrawn within 10 business days of becoming due and payable; commission checks belong in the operating account.

questions.question_text: Under Montana rules, how soon must a broker deposit earnest money into the trust account after receipt?
questions.explanation: Per ARM 24.210.427(4) (which replaced former ARM 24.210.426 in February 2026), Montana brokers must deposit monies belonging to others into a trust account within 3 business days, unless otherwise agreed to by the parties in the contract.

questions.question_text: A purchase contract states earnest money will be deposited within 7 days instead of the standard timeline. Is this acceptable?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if the contract specifies otherwise
distractors: No, 3 business days is always required
questions.explanation: While Montana's default rule is 3 business days, the purchase contract may specify an alternative deposit timeline. If the contract addresses timing, its terms govern the deposit deadline.

questions.question_text: Trust funds earned as commission become due to the broker. How quickly must they be withdrawn?
questions.explanation: When trust funds become due to the broker as earned commission, Montana requires withdrawal within 10 business days of the funds becoming due and payable (ARM 24.210.427). Leaving earned funds in the trust account beyond this period is improper.

questions.question_text: How often must a Montana broker reconcile trust account records?
questions.explanation: Montana requires trust account records to be reconciled each month in which the account has activity (ARM 24.210.427(2)). The broker must compare bank statements against their records to ensure all funds are properly accounted for.

questions.question_text: A transaction falls apart and both buyer and seller claim the $10,000 earnest money deposit. What must the broker do?
distractors: Release funds to the seller automatically
Split the funds equally between parties
Return all funds to the buyer immediately
questions.explanation: When earnest money is disputed, a Montana broker cannot unilaterally disburse the funds to either party. The broker must hold the funds until the parties reach agreement or a court orders disbursement.
